_meta.description: Hand-curated boilerplate keyed by CWE ID. Used by --boilerplate to back-fill empty description/remediation fields on findings. Replaces v0.4 'PwnDoc-NG lookup' with a single, version-controlled, offline file. Contributions welcome via PR.

schema.title: Short human-readable title for the weakness
schema.description: 1-3 sentence neutral definition of the weakness
schema.remediation: Concrete, prioritized fix steps in plain language
schema.references: Canonical pointers (OWASP, MITRE, NIST). Always include the MITRE CWE page.

CWE-22.title: Path Traversal
CWE-22.description: The application constructs filesystem paths from user-controlled input without properly canonicalizing or constraining them, allowing an attacker to access files outside the intended directory.
CWE-22.remediation: Reject input containing '..', '~', and absolute path prefixes. Resolve the requested path with the language's canonicalization API (e.g., `Path.resolve()`, `realpath()`) and verify the result lives under a strict allowlist root before reading.

CWE-77.title: Command Injection
CWE-77.description: User-controlled input is incorporated into a shell command or command interpreter without sanitization, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary commands on the host.
CWE-77.remediation: Never construct shell commands by string concatenation. Use language-native APIs that take an argument array (`subprocess.run([...], shell=False)`, `execve`). If shelling is unavoidable, strictly allowlist the operand and reject any character outside the allowlist.

CWE-78.title: OS Command Injection
CWE-78.description: Specialization of CWE-77: user input is inserted into OS-level command execution (e.g., `system()`, backticks, `exec()`) without sanitization, allowing arbitrary OS command execution under the application's user.
CWE-78.remediation: Use parameterized OS APIs that accept argument arrays (e.g., `subprocess.run(['cmd', arg], shell=False)`). Drop privileges before invoking subprocesses. Run the application as a non-root user.

CWE-79.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
CWE-79.description: User-supplied input is reflected in a server response (reflected XSS) or stored and later rendered (stored XSS) without context-appropriate output encoding, allowing an attacker to execute JavaScript in another user's browser.
CWE-79.remediation: Apply context-aware output encoding at the rendering layer — HTML body, HTML attribute, JavaScript context, URL context each require different encoding. Use the templating engine's auto-escape feature (e.g., Jinja `|e`, React JSX). Add a strict `Content-Security-Policy` header that forbids inline scripts.

CWE-89.title: SQL Injection
CWE-89.description: User-supplied input is concatenated into a SQL query without separation between code and data, allowing an attacker to alter the query's intent — typically to read, modify, or destroy database contents.
CWE-89.remediation: Rewrite every data-access path to use parameterized queries (prepared statements with bound parameters, or an ORM's bound-parameter API). Never build SQL by string concatenation. Apply the principle of least privilege to the database user the application connects with.

CWE-94.title: Code Injection
CWE-94.description: User-supplied input is interpreted as code by a language runtime (e.g., `eval()`, `Function()`, template engines with dynamic compilation), allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code in the application's process.
CWE-94.remediation: Eliminate all calls to runtime code evaluation primitives that take user input. Replace template-engine code-execution features with a safe, restricted expression language. If dynamic logic is unavoidable, sandbox it (e.g., a Wasm runtime with no host imports).

CWE-200.title: Information Exposure
CWE-200.description: The application discloses information about its internal state, environment, or stored data to an actor who is not explicitly authorized to receive it — via responses, headers, error messages, debug pages, or files.
CWE-200.remediation: Audit every endpoint and response for over-disclosure. Strip stack traces, framework banners, and internal hostnames from error responses. Disable debug/development endpoints in production. Apply a default-deny information policy.

CWE-209.title: Information Exposure Through Error Message
CWE-209.description: Error messages returned to users contain sensitive details — stack traces, file paths, database queries, framework versions — that aid an attacker in reconnaissance and exploit development.
CWE-209.remediation: In production, log full errors server-side and return generic messages (e.g., 'An error occurred — reference ID: abc123') to the client. Map exceptions to safe user-facing strings in middleware. Verify across every error path including 4xx and 5xx handlers.

CWE-269.title: Improper Privilege Management
CWE-269.description: The application does not properly assign, track, modify, or check user privileges, allowing a user to act with elevated permissions outside their intended role.
CWE-269.remediation: Centralize authorization checks in a single middleware. Use role-based or attribute-based access control consistently. Apply the principle of least privilege to every endpoint and resource. Audit assignment of administrative roles regularly.

CWE-287.title: Improper Authentication
CWE-287.description: The application does not adequately verify a claimed identity before granting access — weak credential checks, missing multi-factor authentication, broken session handling, or accept-on-fail logic.
CWE-287.remediation: Enforce strong password policies (NIST SP 800-63B). Require multi-factor authentication for all administrative and sensitive accounts. Use a vetted authentication library or framework feature; do not roll your own. Rate-limit and lock authentication endpoints to mitigate brute force.

CWE-307.title: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
CWE-307.description: The application does not limit or sufficiently slow the rate of failed authentication attempts, allowing brute-force password guessing or credential-stuffing attacks at scale.
CWE-307.remediation: Implement progressive lockout after N failed attempts (e.g., 5-10) with an exponential back-off. Add CAPTCHA or device-attestation challenges after the threshold. Monitor and alert on high-volume failure patterns. Block known credential-stuffing IP ranges at the edge.

CWE-311.title: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
CWE-311.description: The application stores or transmits sensitive data — credentials, PII, payment data, secrets — without encrypting it, exposing the data to an attacker with read access to the storage or network path.
CWE-311.remediation: Encrypt all sensitive data at rest (per-record envelope encryption with a KMS-managed key). Enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data in transit. Rotate encryption keys per a documented schedule. Inventory every place sensitive data lives.

CWE-319.title: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
CWE-319.description: The application transmits sensitive data over an unencrypted channel — HTTP, plain SMTP, plain FTP, telnet — exposing it to passive interception by anyone on the network path.
CWE-319.remediation: Disable plaintext protocols at the server. Require TLS 1.2+ for every endpoint that handles sensitive data, and HSTS for all browser-facing services. Verify with a TLS scanner (`testssl.sh`, `sslyze`) that no plaintext fallback exists.

CWE-352.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
CWE-352.description: The application accepts state-changing HTTP requests from authenticated users without verifying that the request originated from a legitimate UI flow, allowing an attacker to trigger actions on behalf of a logged-in victim via a malicious cross-origin request.
CWE-352.remediation: Implement a synchronizer token pattern: a unique, unpredictable per-session token included in every state-changing form and verified server-side. For APIs, set `SameSite=Strict` on session cookies and verify `Origin` / `Referer`. Modern frameworks ship this by default — do not disable it.

CWE-434.title: Unrestricted File Upload
CWE-434.description: The application accepts uploaded files without sufficiently validating their type, content, or storage location, allowing an attacker to upload a file (typically a webshell) that the server later executes as code or serves with a dangerous Content-Type.
CWE-434.remediation: Validate uploaded file content via magic-byte sniffing, not the extension or `Content-Type` header. Store uploads outside the web server's document root. Serve uploads via a controller that sets a forced `Content-Disposition: attachment` and the correct narrowed `Content-Type`. Cap file size at the edge.

CWE-502.title: Insecure Deserialization
CWE-502.description: The application deserializes attacker-controlled data using a language feature that can instantiate arbitrary classes or invoke methods during deserialization (Java `ObjectInputStream`, Python `pickle`, PHP `unserialize`, .NET `BinaryFormatter`), allowing remote code execution.
CWE-502.remediation: Replace native-object deserialization with a data-only format (JSON, protobuf) that cannot trigger code paths. If a native serializer is unavoidable, restrict it to a known allowlist of types and sign serialized payloads with HMAC.

CWE-538.title: File and Directory Information Exposure
CWE-538.description: The application discloses information about files or directories that should not be reachable — typically configuration files, version-control directories (`.git/`, `.svn/`), backup files, or temporary editor files served by a misconfigured web server.
CWE-538.remediation: Block access to sensitive paths (`.git/`, `.svn/`, `.env`, `~` swap files, `*.bak`) at the web server or reverse proxy layer. Audit your deployment artifact for files that should never have been packaged.

CWE-601.title: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)
CWE-601.description: The application redirects users to a URL specified in a request parameter without verifying that the destination is on an allowlist, enabling phishing and social-engineering attacks that abuse the application's trusted domain.
CWE-601.remediation: Maintain a strict server-side allowlist of permitted redirect targets. Pass redirect destinations as an index (e.g., `?next=dashboard`) rather than a URL. If a URL parameter is unavoidable, parse it server-side and reject anything outside the allowlist.

CWE-611.title: XML External Entity (XXE)
CWE-611.description: An XML parser is configured to resolve external entity references in untrusted XML input, allowing an attacker to read local files, perform server-side request forgery, or cause denial of service.
CWE-611.remediation: Disable DTD processing and external entity resolution in every XML parser the application uses (set `XMLConstants.FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING` in Java, `defusedxml` in Python, `LIBXML_NONET | LIBXML_DTDLOAD` in PHP). Prefer JSON over XML for new APIs.

CWE-639.title: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (IDOR)
CWE-639.description: The application uses identifiers from user input to fetch or modify resources without verifying that the requesting user is authorized to access the referenced resource — Insecure Direct Object Reference.
CWE-639.remediation: Verify ownership / authorization on every resource access path, not just the navigation path. Replace sequential numeric IDs with unpredictable identifiers (UUIDv4) so enumeration is infeasible. Centralize the authorization check in a middleware shared by every controller.

CWE-693.title: Missing Security Headers / Protection Mechanism Failure
CWE-693.description: The application does not send one or more recommended HTTP security headers (`Strict-Transport-Security`, `Content-Security-Policy`, `X-Content-Type-Options`, `Referrer-Policy`), leaving users exposed to risks (downgrade, XSS, MIME-sniffing, leakage) the headers are designed to mitigate.
CWE-693.remediation: Add the recommended OWASP Secure Headers set at the edge / framework layer. Verify with `securityheaders.com` or `testssl.sh`. Treat the header set as part of the framework defaults, not opt-in.

CWE-732.title: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
CWE-732.description: The application or its installer assigns permissions to a critical resource — file, directory, registry key, AD object — that are broader than necessary, allowing an unintended actor to read or modify it.
CWE-732.remediation: Apply the principle of least privilege. Audit the deployment for world-writable files (`find / -perm -o+w`). For Active Directory, run BloodHound and remediate any GenericAll / WriteOwner / WriteDACL edges from non-admin principals to privileged objects.

CWE-798.title: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
CWE-798.description: The source code, build artifact, or configuration contains credentials (passwords, API keys, tokens, private keys) in plaintext, exposing them to anyone with read access to the artifact.
CWE-798.remediation: Remove every hardcoded secret from version control. Rotate them — the compromised values must be considered burned. Load secrets at runtime from a secret-management service (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Kubernetes Secrets with encrypted etcd). Run `gitleaks` or `trufflehog` in CI to prevent regressions.

CWE-862.title: Missing Authorization
CWE-862.description: The application performs a sensitive action (state change, data access, administrative operation) without checking whether the requesting user is authorized to perform it.
CWE-862.remediation: Add an explicit authorization check on every sensitive endpoint, not just the navigation layer. Centralize the check in a middleware so it cannot be forgotten. Treat the absence of an explicit check as a missing test; CI should fail when an endpoint lacks one.

CWE-863.title: Incorrect Authorization
CWE-863.description: The application performs an authorization check but does so incorrectly — wrong subject, wrong resource, wrong action, or with a fail-open default — allowing an unauthorized actor to perform the action.
CWE-863.remediation: Audit every authorization predicate. Default to deny, then explicitly allow. Cover the check with negative tests (e.g., 'low-priv user cannot perform admin action'). Use a uniform policy language (OPA, Casbin) instead of ad-hoc predicates scattered across the codebase.

CWE-918.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
CWE-918.description: The application makes an outbound HTTP (or other protocol) request to a URL supplied or influenced by user input, allowing an attacker to reach internal services (cloud metadata endpoints, internal admin panels) from the server's network position.
CWE-918.remediation: Maintain a strict allowlist of permitted outbound hosts. Block RFC 1918, loopback, link-local (169.254/16), and cloud metadata addresses at the network layer for outbound calls from the application tier. Use an egress proxy that enforces the allowlist independently of application code.

CWE-1021.title: UI Redress (Clickjacking)
CWE-1021.description: The application can be embedded inside an attacker-controlled page via a frame or iframe, allowing the attacker to overlay invisible UI on top of the application and trick the user into clicking on sensitive controls.
CWE-1021.remediation: Send `Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'none'` (or a narrow allowlist) on every page. Legacy fallback: send `X-Frame-Options: DENY`. Verify with `securityheaders.com`.
